topic: Alignment - Checking and Asserting Pointer Alignment

turns.content: I'm so frustrated that my program just segfaulted because I tried to deref a pointer without checking if it was actually aligned. Why does Rust force me to do this manually when the hardware seems to handle it automatically? Isn't the compiler supposed to catch these errors before runtime?

The reason you have to check alignment yourself is that the CPU instruction set doesn't guarantee every memory address is safe for every operation, and Rust's safety model requires explicit guarantees instead of trusting implicit behavior. Specifically, when you perform an unsafe dereference using a pointer like &mut [T] or Raw pointers from std::ptr, the hardware might execute a misaligned access that triggers a fault on some architectures, but Rust cannot assume this won't happen because it needs to allow you to write code that works across different platforms. The solution is to use the ptr.is_aligned() method available since stable 1.79 which checks if a pointer address satisfies the standard alignment requirements for your architecture, or use the newer ptr::is_aligned_to(N) trait to verify custom alignments like 8-byte or 16-byte boundaries required by SIMD instructions.
That sounds complicated, but I guess I can just add an assert_eq!(addr % align, 0) check before every unsafe block? Does that actually prevent the segfault?

Adding a runtime check like assert_eq!(ptr.addr() % align, 0) or using ptr::is_aligned_to(N) will indeed catch misaligned addresses at runtime instead of letting them cause silent crashes or hardware faults, but it's better to use the standard library methods designed for this purpose. The Rust standard library provides specialized functions in the std::ptr module that handle these checks efficiently without requiring you to manually calculate modulo operations or cast pointers to usize. You should also consider using macro helpers or traits like Align that encapsulate this logic so your code remains readable and less error-prone when dealing with complex FFI scenarios where foreign function interfaces might have strict alignment requirements.

Okay, I'll use ptr::is_aligned_to(N) instead of manual modulo checks. One last question though, how does this relate to the (ptr + align - 1) & !(align - 1) pattern I saw in some blog posts about aligning memory?

The bitwise expression you mentioned is a mathematical trick for rounding up an address to the next multiple of its alignment without expensive division operations, which is useful when manually calculating aligned buffers or offsets. While ptr::is_aligned_to(N) already handles verification correctly, that arithmetic pattern helps you compute aligned addresses directly if you need to construct new pointers or adjust existing ones before passing them to unsafe code blocks. In practice, for most safety-critical applications involving FFI or SIMD, relying on the built-in alignment checks from std::ptr is more robust than implementing your own bit manipulation logic.
